                        "{1!r}".format(self.__class__.__name__, param_name))
                value = kwargs.pop(param_name)
                if value is None:
                    continue
                # We use quantity_asanyarray here instead of np.asanyarray
                # because if any of the arguments are quantities, we need
                # to return a Quantity object not a plain Numpy array.
                value = quantity_asanyarray(value, dtype=float)
                params.add(param_name)
                self._initialize_parameter_value(param_name, value)
        # Now deal with case where param_name is not supplied by args or kwargs
        for param_name in self.param_names:
            if param_name not in params:
                self._initialize_parameter_value(param_name, None)

        if kwargs:
            # If any keyword arguments were left over at this point they are
            # invalid--the base class should only be passed the parameter
            # values, constraints, and param_dim
            for kwarg in kwargs:
                # Just raise an error on the first unrecognized argument
                raise TypeError(
                    '{0}.__init__() got an unrecognized parameter '
                    '{1!r}'.format(self.__class__.__name__, kwarg))

        # Determine the number of model sets: If the model_set_axis is
        # None then there is just one parameter set; otherwise it is determined
        # by the size of that axis on the first parameter--if the other
        # parameters don't have the right number of axes or the sizes of their
        # model_set_axis don't match an error is raised
        if model_set_axis is not False and n_models != 1 and params:
            max_ndim = 0
            if model_set_axis < 0:
                min_ndim = abs(model_set_axis)
            else:
                min_ndim = model_set_axis + 1

            for name in self.param_names:
                value = getattr(self, name)
                param_ndim = np.ndim(value)
                if param_ndim < min_ndim:
                    raise InputParameterError(
                        "All parameter values must be arrays of dimension "
                        "at least {0} for model_set_axis={1} (the value "
                        "given for {2!r} is only {3}-dimensional)".format(
                            min_ndim, model_set_axis, name, param_ndim))

                max_ndim = max(max_ndim, param_ndim)

                if n_models is None:
                    # Use the dimensions of the first parameter to determine
                    # the number of model sets
                    n_models = value.shape[model_set_axis]
                elif value.shape[model_set_axis] != n_models:
                    raise InputParameterError(
                        "Inconsistent dimensions for parameter {0!r} for "
                        "{1} model sets.  The length of axis {2} must be the "
                        "same for all input parameter values".format(
                            name, n_models, model_set_axis))

            self._check_param_broadcast(max_ndim)
        else:
            if n_models is None:
                n_models = 1

            self._check_param_broadcast(None)

        self._n_models = n_models
        # now validate parameters
        for name in params:
            param = getattr(self, name)
            if param._validator is not None:
                param._validator(self, param.value)

    def _initialize_parameter_value(self, param_name, value):
        """Mostly deals with consistency checks and determining unit issues."""
        if isinstance(value, Parameter):
            self.__dict__[param_name] = value
            return
        param = getattr(self, param_name)
        # Use default if value is not provided
        if value is None:
            default = param.default
            if default is None:
                # No value was supplied for the parameter and the
                # parameter does not have a default, therefore the model
                # is underspecified
                raise TypeError("{0}.__init__() requires a value for parameter "
                                "{1!r}".format(self.__class__.__name__, param_name))
            value = default
            unit = param.unit
        else:
            if isinstance(value, Quantity):
                unit = value.unit
                value = value.value
            else:
                unit = None
        if unit is None and param.unit is not None:
            raise InputParameterError(
                "{0}.__init__() requires a Quantity for parameter "
                "{1!r}".format(self.__class__.__name__, param_name))
        param._unit = unit
        param.internal_unit = None
        if param._setter is not None:
            if unit is not None:
                _val = param._setter(value * unit)
            else:
                _val = param._setter(value)
            if isinstance(_val, Quantity):
                param.internal_unit = _val.unit
                param._internal_value = np.array(_val.value)
            else:
                param.internal_unit = None
                param._internal_value = np.array(_val)
        else:
            param._value = np.array(value)

    def _initialize_slices(self):

        param_metrics = self._param_metrics
        total_size = 0

        for name in self.param_names:
            param = getattr(self, name)
            value = param.value
            param_size = np.size(value)
            param_shape = np.shape(value)
            param_slice = slice(total_size, total_size + param_size)
            param_metrics[name]['slice'] = param_slice
            param_metrics[name]['shape'] = param_shape
            param_metrics[name]['size'] = param_size
            total_size += param_size
        self._parameters = np.empty(total_size, dtype=np.float64)

    def _parameters_to_array(self):
        # Now set the parameter values (this will also fill
        # self._parameters)
        param_metrics = self._param_metrics
        for name in self.param_names:
            param = getattr(self, name)
            value = param.value
            if not isinstance(value, np.ndarray):
                value = np.array([value])
            self._parameters[param_metrics[name]['slice']] = value.ravel()

        # Finally validate all the parameters; we do this last so that
        # validators that depend on one of the other parameters' values will
        # work

    def _array_to_parameters(self):
        param_metrics = self._param_metrics
        for name in self.param_names:
            param = getattr(self, name)
            value = self._parameters[param_metrics[name]['slice']]
            value.shape = param_metrics[name]['shape']
            param.value = value

    def _check_param_broadcast(self, max_ndim):
        """
        This subroutine checks that all parameter arrays can be broadcast
        against each other, and determines the shapes parameters must have in
        order to broadcast correctly.

        If model_set_axis is None this merely checks that the parameters
        broadcast and returns an empty dict if so.  This mode is only used for
        single model sets.
        """
        all_shapes = []
        model_set_axis = self._model_set_axis

        for name in self.param_names:
            param = getattr(self, name)
            value = param.value
            param_shape = np.shape(value)
            param_ndim = len(param_shape)
            if max_ndim is not None and param_ndim < max_ndim:
                # All arrays have the same number of dimensions up to the
                # model_set_axis dimension, but after that they may have a
                # different number of trailing axes.  The number of trailing
                # axes must be extended for mutual compatibility.  For example
                # if max_ndim = 3 and model_set_axis = 0, an array with the
                # shape (2, 2) must be extended to (2, 1, 2).  However, an
                # array with shape (2,) is extended to (2, 1).
                new_axes = (1,) * (max_ndim - param_ndim)

                if model_set_axis < 0:
                    # Just need to prepend axes to make up the difference
                    broadcast_shape = new_axes + param_shape
                else:
                    broadcast_shape = (param_shape[:model_set_axis + 1] +
                                       new_axes +
                                       param_shape[model_set_axis + 1:])
                self._param_metrics[name]['broadcast_shape'] = broadcast_shape
                all_shapes.append(broadcast_shape)
            else:
                all_shapes.append(param_shape)

        # Now check mutual broadcastability of all shapes
        try:
            check_broadcast(*all_shapes)
        except IncompatibleShapeError as exc:
            shape_a, shape_a_idx, shape_b, shape_b_idx = exc.args
            param_a = self.param_names[shape_a_idx]
            param_b = self.param_names[shape_b_idx]

            raise InputParameterError(
                "Parameter {0!r} of shape {1!r} cannot be broadcast with "
                "parameter {2!r} of shape {3!r}.  All parameter arrays "
                "must have shapes that are mutually compatible according "
                "to the broadcasting rules.".format(param_a, shape_a,
                                                    param_b, shape_b))

    def _param_sets(self, raw=False, units=False):
        """
        Implementation of the Model.param_sets property.

        This internal implementation has a ``raw`` argument which controls
        whether or not to return the raw parameter values (i.e. the values that
        are actually stored in the ._parameters array, as opposed to the values
        displayed to users.  In most cases these are one in the same but there
        are currently a few exceptions.

        Note: This is notably an overcomplicated device and may be removed
        entirely in the near future.
        """

        values = []
        shapes = []
        for name in self.param_names:
            param = getattr(self, name)

            if raw and param._setter:
                value = param._internal_value
            else:
                value = param.value

            broadcast_shape = self._param_metrics[name].get('broadcast_shape')
            if broadcast_shape is not None:
                value = value.reshape(broadcast_shape)

            shapes.append(np.shape(value))

            if len(self) == 1:
                # Add a single param set axis to the parameter's value (thus
                # converting scalars to shape (1,) array values) for
                # consistency
                value = np.array([value])

            if units:
                if raw and param.internal_unit is not None:
                    unit = param.internal_unit
                else:
                    unit = param.unit
                if unit is not None:
                    value = Quantity(value, unit)

            values.append(value)

        if len(set(shapes)) != 1 or units:
            # If the parameters are not all the same shape, converting to an
            # array is going to produce an object array
            # However the way Numpy creates object arrays is tricky in that it
            # will recurse into array objects in the list and break them up
            # into separate objects.  Doing things this way ensures a 1-D
            # object array the elements of which are the individual parameter
            # arrays.  There's not much reason to do this over returning a list
            # except for consistency
            psets = np.empty(len(values), dtype=object)
            psets[:] = values
            return psets

        return np.array(values)

    def _format_repr(self, args=[], kwargs={}, defaults={}):
        """
        Internal implementation of ``__repr__``.

        This is separated out for ease of use by subclasses that wish to
        override the default ``__repr__`` while keeping the same basic
        formatting.
        """

        parts = [repr(a) for a in args]

        parts.extend(
            f"{name}={param_repr_oneline(getattr(self, name))}"
            for name in self.param_names)

        if self.name is not None:
            parts.append(f'name={self.name!r}')

        for kwarg, value in kwargs.items():
            if kwarg in defaults and defaults[kwarg] == value:
                continue
            parts.append(f'{kwarg}={value!r}')

        if len(self) > 1:
            parts.append(f"n_models={len(self)}")

        return f"<{self.__class__.__name__}({', '.join(parts)})>"

    def _format_str(self, keywords=[], defaults={}):
        """
        Internal implementation of ``__str__``.

        This is separated out for ease of use by subclasses that wish to
        override the default ``__str__`` while keeping the same basic
        formatting.
        """

        default_keywords = [
            ('Model', self.__class__.__name__),
            ('Name', self.name),
            ('Inputs', self.inputs),
            ('Outputs', self.outputs),
            ('Model set size', len(self))
        ]

        parts = [f'{keyword}: {value}'
                 for keyword, value in default_keywords
                 if value is not None]

        for keyword, value in keywords:
            if keyword.lower() in defaults and defaults[keyword.lower()] == value:
                continue
            parts.append(f'{keyword}: {value}')
        parts.append('Parameters:')

        if len(self) == 1:
            columns = [[getattr(self, name).value]
                       for name in self.param_names]
        else:
            columns = [getattr(self, name).value
                       for name in self.param_names]

        if columns:
            param_table = Table(columns, names=self.param_names)
            # Set units on the columns
            for name in self.param_names:
                param_table[name].unit = getattr(self, name).unit
            parts.append(indent(str(param_table), width=4))

        return '\n'.join(parts)

class Mapping(FittableModel):
    """
    Allows inputs to be reordered, duplicated or dropped.

    Parameters
    ----------
    mapping : tuple
        A tuple of integers representing indices of the inputs to this model
        to return and in what order to return them.  See
        :ref:`astropy:compound-model-mappings` for more details.
    n_inputs : int
        Number of inputs; if `None` (default) then ``max(mapping) + 1`` is
        used (i.e. the highest input index used in the mapping).
    name : str, optional
        A human-friendly name associated with this model instance
        (particularly useful for identifying the individual components of a
        compound model).
    meta : dict-like
        Free-form metadata to associate with this model.

    Raises
    ------
    TypeError
        Raised when number of inputs is less that ``max(mapping)``.

    Examples
    --------

    >>> from astropy.modeling.models import Polynomial2D, Shift, Mapping
    >>> poly1 = Polynomial2D(1, c0_0=1, c1_0=2, c0_1=3)
    >>> poly2 = Polynomial2D(1, c0_0=1, c1_0=2.4, c0_1=2.1)
    >>> model = (Shift(1) & Shift(2)) | Mapping((0, 1, 0, 1)) | (poly1 & poly2)
    >>> model(1, 2)  # doctest: +FLOAT_CMP
    (17.0, 14.2)
    """
    linear = True  # FittableModel is non-linear by default

    def __init__(self, mapping, n_inputs=None, name=None, meta=None):
        self._inputs = ()
        self._outputs = ()
        if n_inputs is None:
            self._n_inputs = max(mapping) + 1
        else:
            self._n_inputs = n_inputs

        self._n_outputs = len(mapping)
        super().__init__(name=name, meta=meta)

        self.inputs = tuple('x' + str(idx) for idx in range(self._n_inputs))
        self.outputs = tuple('x' + str(idx) for idx in range(self._n_outputs))

        self._mapping = mapping
        self._input_units_strict = {key: False for key in self._inputs}
        self._input_units_allow_dimensionless = {key: False for key in self._inputs}

    @property
    def n_inputs(self):
        return self._n_inputs

    @property
    def n_outputs(self):
        return self._n_outputs

    @property
    def mapping(self):
        """Integers representing indices of the inputs."""
        return self._mapping

    def __repr__(self):
        if self.name is None:
            return f'<Mapping({self.mapping})>'
        return f'<Mapping({self.mapping}, name={self.name!r})>'

    def evaluate(self, *args):
        if len(args) != self.n_inputs:
            name = self.name if self.name is not None else "Mapping"

            raise TypeError(f'{name} expects {self.n_inputs} inputs; got {len(args)}')

        result = tuple(args[idx] for idx in self._mapping)

        if self.n_outputs == 1:
            return result[0]

        return result

    @property
    def inverse(self):
        """
        A `Mapping` representing the inverse of the current mapping.

        Raises
        ------
        `NotImplementedError`
            An inverse does no exist on mappings that drop some of its inputs
            (there is then no way to reconstruct the inputs that were dropped).
        """

        try:
            mapping = tuple(self.mapping.index(idx)
                            for idx in range(self.n_inputs))
        except ValueError:
            raise NotImplementedError(
                "Mappings such as {} that drop one or more of their inputs "
                "are not invertible at this time.".format(self.mapping))

        inv = self.__class__(mapping)
        inv._inputs = self._outputs
        inv._outputs = self._inputs
        inv._n_inputs = len(inv._inputs)
        inv._n_outputs = len(inv._outputs)
        return inv

Observation: {"node": "astropy/modeling/separable.py:separability_matrix", "code": "def separability_matrix(transform):
    """
    Compute the correlation between outputs and inputs.

    Parameters
    ----------
    transform : `~astropy.modeling.core.Model`
        A (compound) model.

    Returns
    -------
    separable_matrix : ndarray
        A boolean correlation matrix of shape (n_outputs, n_inputs).
        Indicates the dependence of outputs on inputs. For completely
        independent outputs, the diagonal elements are True and
        off-diagonal elements are False.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from astropy.modeling.models import Shift, Scale, Rotation2D, Polynomial2D
    >>> separability_matrix(Shift(1) & Shift(2) | Scale(1) & Scale(2))
        array([[ True, False], [False,  True]]...)
    >>> separability_matrix(Shift(1) & Shift(2) | Rotation2D(2))
        array([[ True,  True], [ True,  True]]...)
    >>> separability_matrix(Shift(1) & Shift(2) | Mapping([0, 1, 0, 1]) | \
        Polynomial2D(1) & Polynomial2D(2))
        array([[ True,  True], [ True,  True]]...)
    >>> separability_matrix(Shift(1) & Shift(2) | Mapping([0, 1, 0, 1]))
        array([[ True, False], [False,  True], [ True, False], [False,  True]]...)

    """
    if transform.n_inputs == 1 and transform.n_outputs > 1:
        return np.ones((transform.n_outputs, transform.n_inputs),
                       dtype=np.bool_)
    separable_matrix = _separable(transform)
    separable_matrix = np.where(separable_matrix != 0, True, False)
    return separable_matrix", "type": "function"}

What I needed and why:
- separability_matrix and the helper that builds the coordinate / separability matrices (_cstack, _coord_matrix, _separable) because the reported output is a matrix computed by that code.
- CompoundModel and Model because separability_matrix recurses into CompoundModel nodes and uses _calculate_separability_matrix hook.
- Mapping because separable._coord_matrix has special handling for Mapping.

1c) Important code I inspected
- astropy/modeling/separable.py — full module. Key parts:
  - separability_matrix() → calls _separable(transform)
  - _separable(transform):
    - tries transform._calculate_separability_matrix()
    - if transform is CompoundModel: recursively compute for transform.left/right and call operator function: _operators[transform.op]
    - for a leaf Model: return _coord_matrix(model, 'left', model.n_outputs)
  - _cstack(left, right) — handles '&' operator (horizontal stacking / join). This constructs noutp then builds cleft and cright and finally returns np.hstack([cleft, cright])
- astropy/modeling/core.py — CompoundModel: how left/right and op are stored, n_inputs/n_outputs, how leaf vs compound child types are handled.
- astropy/modeling/mappings.py — Mapping class (special-case in _coord_matrix).

1d) Clear statement of the bug and primary responsible method/class
- Bug: separability_matrix (and the underlying _separable/_cstack logic) computes an incorrect separability matrix when a CompoundModel has a right child that is itself a CompoundModel (i.e., nested compound on the right). Concretely: when the right child is compound, _separable(transform.right) returns a coord_matrix ndarray and _cstack treats that ndarray incorrectly by filling the right-hand block with scalar 1s instead of the right child's coord_matrix. This produces spurious dependencies between outputs and inputs.
- Primary responsible function: separable._cstack (in astropy/modeling/separable.py). The incorrect line is in the branch handling "if right is not a Model" (i.e., right is an ndarray) where cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1 is used instead of assigning the right array.

Evidence (from code):
- In _cstack (separable.py):
  - For left not Model: cleft[: left.shape[0], : left.shape[1]] = left  (this is correct — it copies the left coord matrix).
  - For right not Model: cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1  (this is wrong — should copy right).

This is exactly why
- separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & (Linear1D & Linear1D)) returns the wrong matrix: the right-hand CompoundModel's separability matrix is replaced by ones (or by a block of 1s), making outputs appear to depend on both inputs.

Checkpoint 3 — Map the architecture

3a) For each component class used in separable.py, I retrieved their definitions and enumerated class-level attributes and constants.

- Model (astropy/modeling/core.py: class Model):
  - Many class-level attributes; of interest for separability logic:
    - n_inputs, n_outputs (class attributes / properties)
    - _separable (default None) — property Model.separable uses _separable or raises NotImplementedError.
    - _calculate_separability_matrix(self) — hook: default returns NotImplemented (so separable._separable uses it if implemented in some models).
  - No hardcoded numbers in Model relevant to separability, but important behaviour:
    - _calculate_separability_matrix default returns NotImplemented → triggers default recursive behaviour in separable._separable for CompoundModel nodes.
    - Model.separable property reads _separable (some Model subclasses define _separable True/False).

- CompoundModel (astropy/modeling/core.py: class CompoundModel):
  - Parent: Model
  - Key attributes and behaviour relevant to separability:
    - op, left, right (set at __init__)
    - n_inputs, n_outputs (set based on operator)
    - For op == '&' (join): n_inputs = left.n_inputs + right.n_inputs; n_outputs = left.n_outputs + right.n_outputs
    - left/right can be Model or CompoundModel
    - _calculate_separability_matrix is not overridden in CompoundModel (so it uses Model._calculate_separability_matrix default NotImplemented)
    - Many methods for mapping inputs/outputs, traverse_postorder, etc. No hardcoded numeric offsets other than the arithmetic to compute sums of n_inputs/n_outputs for '&'.
  - Methods defined (not exhaustive; focused on methods that separable.py will interact with):
    - __init__, evaluate/_evaluate, inputs_map(), outputs_map(), n_inputs/n_outputs property setters, traverse_postorder, _make_leaflist, _param mapping functions, etc.

- Mapping (astropy/modeling/mappings.py: class Mapping):
  - Parent: FittableModel (-> Model)
  - class-level attributes relevant:
    - linear = True
  - In __init__ it sets:
    - self._n_inputs = max(mapping) + 1 (or provided n_inputs)
    - self._n_outputs = len(mapping)
    - self.inputs and outputs set to tuples like ('x0', 'x1', ...)
    - self._input_units_strict and _input_units_allow_dimensionless set to dicts of False for each input
  - Methods:
    - evaluate(*args) (returns tuple selecting args by mapping)
    - inverse (constructs inverse Mapping if invertible)
  - Hardcoded numbers:
    - none beyond computing mapping sizes via max(mapping) + 1 and len(mapping). Those are correct: they are dynamically computed.

3a - Hardcoded numeric values to note (from component classes and separable.py):
- separable._coord_matrix uses shapes and does mat[i,i] = 1 for separable models (assumes inputs == outputs ordering). The numeric assignments are index-based (no magic constants). They encode the assumption that for separable models the i-th output depends only on the i-th input (typical for 1D or mapping).
- separable._cstack computes noutp = lnout + rnout (sum of outputs). This is the correct arithmetic for concatenating outputs.
- In Mapping.__init__, n_inputs = max(mapping) + 1 (dynamic).
- In Projection / Pix2Sky classes (projections.py), many classes set _separable True/False; for Pix2SkyProjection in general _separable = False in Projection base class, but many specific projections (Cylindrical, etc.) set _separable = True. For TAN (Gnomonic) Pix2Sky_Gnomonic class does not explicitly set _separable, it inherits Projection._separable = False — but that's not central to this bug.

3b) Parent class of the primary method
- The primary function at fault is _cstack in separable.py (module-level function), it is not part of a class. Its "parent" in architecture terms is the separability module, which depends on Model and CompoundModel. CompoundModel inherits from Model. I retrieved Model earlier and enumerated methods the parent defines (lots of methods). The separable code uses:
  - transform._calculate_separability_matrix() (Model hook)
  - isinstance(transform, CompoundModel) to branch recursion
  - transform.left / transform.right (CompoundModel attributes)
  None of those methods need to be overridden by the CompoundModel for this fix; the bug is purely in separable._cstack.

3c) Sibling classes: other models using _separable or custom _calculate_separability_matrix
- I searched for _calculate_separability_matrix overrides: none other than the default in Model; a test class in tests had an override (for testing). No model appears to implement a custom separability matrix hook, so the recursive default logic must correctly combine children components via operators in _operators; only the implementation of these operators must be correct. Sibling CompoundModel usage shows similar patterns; existing correct behaviour for non-nested compound sequences (left and right are Model objects or arrays in the expected shape) was working; the failure manifests when right child is an ndarray returned by recursive call but is handled incorrectly by _cstack's right-not-Model branch.

3d) Call graph for the primary function
- separability_matrix(transform) → _separable(transform)
  - For leaf Model: _coord_matrix(model, 'left', transform.n_outputs)
  - For CompoundModel: sepleft = _separable(transform.left); sepright = _separable(transform.right); return _operators[transform.op](sepleft, sepright)
- For op == '&': _operators['&'] is _cstack
  - _cstack consumes left and right which may be Model objects or ndarray coordinate matrices and returns horizontally-stacked matrix (np.hstack([cleft, cright])).

Checkpoint 4 — Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (single-line correct bug):
- In astropy/modeling/separable.py, function _cstack, replace the assignment
    cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1
  with
    cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = right

Why: When right is an ndarray (returned by recursive _separable), we must copy the right child's coord_matrix into the bottom-right block of cright, not fill it with ones. The left-not-Model branch already copies the left array into cleft[: left.shape[0], : left.shape[1]] = left. The right branch was inconsistent.

Patch (context):
Replace in separable.py (function _cstack), the block:

    if isinstance(right, Model):
        cright = _coord_matrix(right, 'right', noutp)
    else:
        cright = np.zeros((noutp, right.shape[1]))
        cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1

with:

    if isinstance(right, Model):
        cright = _coord_matrix(right, 'right', noutp)
    else:
        cright = np.zeros((noutp, right.shape[1]))
        cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = right

This is the complete fix for the incorrect treatment of right ndarray.

Now go through the required questions.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence: It removes the incorrect assumption that when a right subtree returns a coord_matrix array (ndarray) it should be treated as "all ones" (i.e., non-separable across all inputs) instead of using the actual coord_matrix returned by the recursive computation.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The erroneous hardcoded value was the assignment of 1 to the right block:
    cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1
  This encodes the assumption that the right block should be filled with ones (representing full dependency) rather than being copied from the right child's coord_matrix. Changing to assign "right" makes the right block dynamic and correct.
- Other hardcoded/assumed numeric constructions in separable.py that I reviewed:
  - In _coord_matrix, when a Model is not separable, it fills block with 1 (matrices of ones) for that model's output block — this is correct: non-separable leaf models produce ones indicating each output depends on all inputs.
  - In _cstack, computing noutp = lnout + rnout is arithmetic consistent with concatenation; it remains correct.
  - No other hardcoded constants in these functions encode the broken assumption.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse operation here is not read/write but symmetry of operator handling across left/right children, and also the other operator functions.
- I checked the other operator function for composition (_cdot) and arithmetic operators:
  - _cdot has a swap 'left, right = right, left' at the start (intended to match dot semantics), and it builds cleft/cright via _coord_matrix when inputs are Model or uses the ndarray passed through; it uses np.dot(cleft, cright). I didn't find analogous mistaken assignment in _cdot.
  - _arith_oper always returns ones (non-separable) — that is intended (arithmetic combination produces non-separable outputs).
- Does this fix require changing any reading counterpart? Separability computation has two exported functions: is_separable and separability_matrix; both call the same _separable helper. The fix updates _cstack used by both, so both functions will be corrected automatically. There is no separate "write" path to update.
- Check whether Model._calculate_separability_matrix hook interaction is affected: the Model hook returns NotImplemented by default; some models could override it. If a model returned an ndarray, _separable expects that. The fix is consistent with that expectation: we now properly copy an ndarray returned by a child.
- Conclusion: No additional reverse/paired method needs to be added.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Methods in separable.py:
  - _compute_n_outputs: unchanged and still correct.
  - _arith_oper: unchanged and intended result (returns ones) still correct.
  - _coord_matrix: unchanged.
  - _cstack: now correct in both left and right ndarray cases: left-not-Model copies left array; right-not-Model copies right array. This restores expected behaviour for nested compound models. No other methods rely on the prior (wrong) behaviour.
  - _cdot: unchanged — composition logic unaffected.
  - _separable: unchanged — will now get correct levers from _cstack result.
- Methods in CompoundModel / Model:
  - No change needed. They were correct; the bug was entirely in separable._cstack.
- I substituted a concrete example to validate the fix:
  - Given cm = Linear1D & Linear1D
    - _separable(cm) returns ndarray [[1,0],[0,1]] (as before)
  - Consider top-level transform = Pix2Sky_TAN() & cm
    - In _cstack: left is Model → cleft = _coord_matrix(Pix2Sky_TAN,...)
      right is ndarray → cright created and assigns right (not ones) into bottom-right block.
    - The final np.hstack yields the 4x4 matrix with correct block structure where the cm outputs are mapped to the two rightmost input axes only.
  - Thus separability_matrix(transform) becomes the expected matrix in the bug report.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new method is required. The change is a single-line correction in _cstack. I read the parent/related methods (Model._calculate_separability_matrix hook, CompoundModel logic, Mapping logic) and found no other missing overrides required by the fix. Parent methods are compatible with the change because the recursive protocol already returns ndarray coordinate matrices for compound subtrees and the operator functions are meant to consume such ndarrays. The only missing piece was that _cstack did not actually copy the ndarray into the overall matrix in the right-not-Model case.

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (complete analysis and patch guidance)

Root cause
- In separable._cstack (operator '&' join) the implementation handled the case "right is not a Model" incorrectly: instead of copying the right child's coord_matrix ndarray into the bottom-right block of the overall matrix, it filled that block with scalar 1 values. This wrong assignment makes nested compound right children appear to make their outputs depend on all inputs, producing incorrect separability matrices for nested compound models on the right.

Every location that needs to change
- Single location: astropy/modeling/separable.py
  - function: _cstack
  - current code (buggy):
    cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1
  - fix:
    cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = right

What the current code does, what assumption it encodes, and what the fix should be:
- Current code: When right child is an ndarray, it fills the bottom-right block with ones (scalar 1).
  - Assumption it encoded: right child being ndarray should be treated as "fully dependent" (all ones), i.e., treat the right child's outputs as depending on every input.
  - Why that is wrong: the ndarray returned by recursive _separable contains the actual dependency matrix for that subtree and must be copied into the parent block; filling with ones discards that information.
- Fix: copy the ndarray into the correct block as the left-not-Model branch already does.

Hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- No other hardcoded numerical constants require changing. The only problematic literal 1 is the incorrect assignment in _cstack; replacing it with assignment of the dynamic ndarray right fixes the problem.

Methods to be added or overridden
- None required. The correct behaviour is achieved by fixing the operator function _cstack. No new Model / CompoundModel methods are required.

Patch (single-line change)

In astropy/modeling/separable.py, edit the function _cstack:

Before (buggy):
```
    if isinstance(right, Model):
        cright = _coord_matrix(right, 'right', noutp)
    else:
        cright = np.zeros((noutp, right.shape[1]))
        cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1
```

After (fixed):
```
    if isinstance(right, Model):
        cright = _coord_matrix(right, 'right', noutp)
    else:
        cright = np.zeros((noutp, right.shape[1]))
        cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = right
```

Rationale and test
- This mirrors how the left-not-Model branch is handled and preserves the right subtree's computed coordinate matrix.
- Tests to validate:
  - Reproduce the original example in the issue:
    - cm = Linear1D(...) & Linear1D(...)
    - separability_matrix(cm) should be diag True/False (unchanged)
    - separability_matrix(Pix2Sky_TAN() & Linear1D & Linear1D) should be as observed (unchanged)
    - separability_matrix(Pix2Sky_TAN() & cm) should now be the expected form (the previous bug output becomes corrected).
  - Add a unit test: create a nested compound with right child being CompoundModel and assert separability_matrix equals expected composition (i.e., that right child block equals the right child's separability matrix, not a block of ones).

Notes and risk assessment
- The change is minimal and local (single assignment). It restores symmetry with the left-not-Model branch which already copies arrays into the hstack blocks. There is no change to public APIs. The code change is safe and should not break other functionality — it simply uses the right child's ndarray result rather than overwriting it.
- I checked other operator functions (_cdot, _arith_oper) for similar issues; none had the same bug. _cdot correctly uses the ndarray values for left/right and applies np.dot. _arith_oper intentionally returns ones for arithmetic combinations (non-separable).

If you want, I can:
- Produce a proposed git-style patch/diff file.
- Add a regression test snippet suitable for the astropy test suite to assert the corrected behaviour (I can draft a pytest function).
- Run a quick search for similar assignment mistakes in the separable module (I already scanned and did not find another occurrence where a right ndarray is replaced by ones).

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

Summary of what I checked and why
- I inspected the full source of astropy/modeling/separable.py to verify the draft's claims about how operator combinators handle ndarray inputs and to locate the exact offending code. This is necessary to confirm the root cause and to determine whether the proposed single-line fix is correct and complete.
- I inspected astropy/modeling/core.py (Model._calculate_separability_matrix) to confirm the expected contract for operator functions (that they must accept Model instances or ndarrays returned by recursive calls or model hooks).
- I searched the codebase for other occurrences of the same pattern (assigning a scalar into a bottom-right sub-block where an ndarray should be copied) so I could include all affected locations in the revised report.

Findings, line-quoted evidence, and verification of draft assertions

1) Problem-location methods and code verification
- separability_matrix (astropy/modeling/separable.py)
  - Behavior: calls _separable(transform), then converts nonzero values to True/False.
  - Evidence: separability_matrix special-case and call at lines 97-102 and 100-102:
    - Lines 97-102:
      if transform.n_inputs == 1 and transform.n_outputs > 1:
          return np.ones((transform.n_outputs, transform.n_inputs),
                         dtype=np.bool_)
      separable_matrix = _separable(transform)
      separable_matrix = np.where(separable_matrix != 0, True, False)
      return separable_matrix
  - Conclusion: separability_matrix delegates to _separable and expects _separable/operator functions to return numeric matrices (0/1) that are then converted to bool.

- _separable (astropy/modeling/separable.py)
  - Behavior: returns transform._calculate_separability_matrix() if implemented; otherwise for CompoundModel it recursively calls _separable on left and right and uses the operator function mapping.
  - Evidence: lines 304-309:
      if (transform_matrix := transform._calculate_separability_matrix()) is not NotImplemented:

          sepleft = _separable(transform.left)
          sepright = _separable(transform.right)
          return _operators[transform.op](sepleft, sepright)
  - Conclusion: operator functions (e.g., _cstack) must accept either Model objects or ndarrays returned by recursive calls or model hooks.

No other functions rely on the incorrect behavior (I searched for 'cright' and the offending assignment and found it only here). The other operator implementations (_cdot, _arith_oper) have different semantics and are correct for their operators.
- Therefore the proposed fix is correct and minimal; it should not break other code that expects the correct copy semantics.

bug_report.Title: separability_matrix returns incorrect blocks for nested CompoundModels (bug in _cstack)
bug_report.Description: separability_matrix produces incorrect separability blocks when a CompoundModel's right child is itself a CompoundModel (i.e. nested compound on the right). In the nested case the bottom-right block of the separability matrix is filled with True (as if the right subtree depended on all inputs) instead of preserving the right child's actual separability ndarray. The underlying operator combinator for '&' (_cstack in astropy/modeling/separable.py) treats a right-side ndarray incorrectly by writing scalar 1 into the bottom-right sub-block instead of copying the right child's ndarray.
bug_report.RootCause: Logic error in astropy/modeling/separable.py::_cstack: when the right operand is already an ndarray (produced by a recursive _separable call or a model's _calculate_separability_matrix), _cstack creates cright and assigns a scalar 1 into the bottom-right sub-block (cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1), discarding the true right-child matrix. The left-not-Model branch copies the left ndarray correctly (cleft[:left.shape[0], :left.shape[1]] = left), so the two branches are inconsistent; this causes spurious dependencies in the combined separability matrix for nested right-side CompoundModels.
StepsToReproduce: from astropy.modeling import models as m
from astropy.modeling.separable import separability_matrix
cm = m.Linear1D(10) & m.Linear1D(5)
separability_matrix(cm)  # yields a 2x2 diagonal True/False matrix as expected
separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & m.Linear1D(10) & m.Linear1D(5))
# yields a 4x4 matrix with expected separability blocks (right-side linear models independent)
separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & cm)
# observed: bottom-right 2x2 block is all True (incorrect); expected diagonal block
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When combining models with '&', separability_matrix should preserve the separability/coord ndarray of each subtree. For nested CompoundModels the bottom-right block (corresponding to the right subtree) should equal the separability matrix computed for that subtree (not a block of all True).
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: The bottom-right block of the matrix for separability_matrix(Pix2Sky_TAN() & cm) is filled with True values (a full dependency), rather than the right-child's actual separability matrix (a diagonal block). This happens only when the right child is already a CompoundModel/ndarray returned by recursive calls.
bug_report.Suggestions:
Make a single, local change to astropy/modeling/separable.py::_cstack and add a regression test that covers both nested and flattened forms. Concrete steps:

1) Edit astropy/modeling/separable.py::_cstack: in the branch where 'right' is an ndarray, replace the scalar assignment with a copy of the right ndarray. Precisely change the line
   cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1
   to
   cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = right
   This mirrors the left-not-Model branch (which uses cleft[:left.shape[0], :left.shape[1]] = left) and preserves the right subtree's separability matrix returned by recursive calls or by Model._calculate_separability_matrix.

2) Add a regression test (example): create a test file or add to astropy/modeling/tests (e.g. test_separable_matrix_nested.py) with a test function that constructs the reported examples and asserts exact results. For example:

   def test_separability_matrix_nested_right_compound():
       from astropy.modeling import models as m
       from astropy.modeling.separable import separability_matrix
       cm = m.Linear1D(10) & m.Linear1D(5)
       # flattened composition
       mat_flat = separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & m.Linear1D(10) & m.Linear1D(5))
       expected_flat = np.array([[ True,  True, False, False],
                                 [ True,  True, False, False],
                                 [False, False,  True, False],
                                 [False, False, False,  True]])
       assert np.array_equal(mat_flat, expected_flat)

       # nested right-side compound
       mat_nested = separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & cm)
       expected_nested = np.array([[ True,  True, False, False],
                                   [ True,  True, False, False],
                                   [False, False,  True, False],
                                   [False, False, False,  True]])
       assert np.array_equal(mat_nested, expected_nested)

   Use appropriate pytest import and array comparison helpers used by the project (e.g. numpy.testing.assert_array_equal) and include the test in the modeling test suite.

3) Run the test suite. If any test expects the previous incorrect behavior (unlikely), update tests accordingly.

4) Optionally add a clarifying code comment in _cstack explaining both left and right branches accept either Model objects or ndarrays from recursive calls/_calculate_separability_matrix and that both branches must copy ndarray inputs rather than replace them with scalars.

Files/methods to change: astropy/modeling/separable.py::_cstack (single-line replacement as above). Add test in astropy/modeling/tests/test_separable_matrix_nested.py or similar.
bug_report.possible_fix: Replace the incorrect assignment that fills cright's bottom-right sub-block with scalar 1 by copying the right ndarray into that sub-block (i.e. use '= right' instead of '= 1'). This restores symmetry with the left-not-Model branch and preserves the right child's separability matrix returned by recursive calls or model overrides. Add a regression test to cover the nested CompoundModel case (exact test code suggested in Suggestions).

possible_fix_code.astropy.modeling.separable._cstack:
def _cstack(left, right):
    """
    Function corresponding to '&' operation.

    Parameters
    ----------
    left, right : `astropy.modeling.Model` or ndarray
        If input is of an array, it is the output of `coord_matrix`.

    Returns
    -------
    result : ndarray
        Result from this operation.

    """
    noutp = _compute_n_outputs(left, right)

    if isinstance(left, Model):
        cleft = _coord_matrix(left, 'left', noutp)
    else:
        cleft = np.zeros((noutp, left.shape[1]))
        cleft[: left.shape[0], : left.shape[1]] = left
    if isinstance(right, Model):
        cright = _coord_matrix(right, 'right', noutp)
    else:
        cright = np.zeros((noutp, right.shape[1]))
        # FIX: copy the right child's ndarray into the bottom-right corner
        # instead of filling it with scalar 1 which discards its structure.
        cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = right

    return np.hstack([cleft, cright])

from .core import DefaultSplitter
from .fixedwidth import (
    FixedWidth,
    FixedWidthData,
    FixedWidthHeader,
    FixedWidthTwoLineDataSplitter,
)


class SimpleRSTHeader(FixedWidthHeader):
    position_line = 0
    start_line = 1
    splitter_class = DefaultSplitter
    position_char = "="

    def get_fixedwidth_params(self, line):
        vals, starts, ends = super().get_fixedwidth_params(line)
        # The right hand column can be unbounded
        ends[-1] = None
        return vals, starts, ends


class SimpleRSTData(FixedWidthData):
    start_line = 3
    end_line = -1
    splitter_class = FixedWidthTwoLineDataSplitter


class RST(FixedWidth):
    """reStructuredText simple format table.

    See: https://docutils.sourceforge.io/docs/ref/rst/restructuredtext.html#simple-tables

    Example::

        ==== ===== ======
        Col1  Col2  Col3
        ==== ===== ======
          1    2.3  Hello
          2    4.5  Worlds
        ==== ===== ======

    Currently there is no support for reading tables which utilize continuation lines,
    or for ones which define column spans through the use of an additional
    line of dashes in the header.

    """

    _format_name = "rst"
    _description = "reStructuredText simple table"
    data_class = SimpleRSTData
    header_class = SimpleRSTHeader

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)

    def write(self, lines):
        lines = super().write(lines)
        lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]
        return lines

class FixedWidth(basic.Basic):
    """Fixed width table with single header line defining column names and positions.

    Examples::

      # Bar delimiter in header and data

      |  Col1 |   Col2      |  Col3 |
      |  1.2  | hello there |     3 |
      |  2.4  | many words  |     7 |

      # Bar delimiter in header only

      Col1 |   Col2      | Col3
      1.2    hello there    3
      2.4    many words     7

      # No delimiter with column positions specified as input

      Col1       Col2Col3
       1.2hello there   3
       2.4many words    7

    See the :ref:`astropy:fixed_width_gallery` for specific usage examples.

    """

    _format_name = "fixed_width"
    _description = "Fixed width"

    header_class = FixedWidthHeader
    data_class = FixedWidthData

    def __init__(
        self,
        col_starts=None,
        col_ends=None,
        delimiter_pad=" ",
        bookend=True,
        header_rows=None,
    ):
        if header_rows is None:
            header_rows = ["name"]
        super().__init__()
        self.data.splitter.delimiter_pad = delimiter_pad
        self.data.splitter.bookend = bookend
        self.header.col_starts = col_starts
        self.header.col_ends = col_ends
        self.header.header_rows = header_rows
        self.data.header_rows = header_rows
        if self.data.start_line is None:
            self.data.start_line = len(header_rows)


class FixedWidthNoHeaderHeader(FixedWidthHeader):
    """Header reader for fixed with tables with no header line"""

    start_line = None


class FixedWidthNoHeaderData(FixedWidthData):
    """Data reader for fixed width tables with no header line"""

    start_line = 0


class FixedWidthNoHeader(FixedWidth):
    """Fixed width table which has no header line.

    When reading, column names are either input (``names`` keyword) or
    auto-generated.  Column positions are determined either by input
    (``col_starts`` and ``col_stops`` keywords) or by splitting the first data
    line.  In the latter case a ``delimiter`` is required to split the data
    line.

    Examples::

      # Bar delimiter in header and data

      |  1.2  | hello there |     3 |
      |  2.4  | many words  |     7 |

      # Compact table having no delimiter and column positions specified as input

      1.2hello there3
      2.4many words 7

    This class is just a convenience wrapper around the ``FixedWidth`` reader
    but with ``header_start=None`` and ``data_start=0``.

    See the :ref:`astropy:fixed_width_gallery` for specific usage examples.

    """

    _format_name = "fixed_width_no_header"
    _description = "Fixed width with no header"
    header_class = FixedWidthNoHeaderHeader
    data_class = FixedWidthNoHeaderData

    def __init__(self, col_starts=None, col_ends=None, delimiter_pad=" ", bookend=True):
        super().__init__(
            col_starts,
            col_ends,
            delimiter_pad=delimiter_pad,
            bookend=bookend,
            header_rows=[],
        )


class FixedWidthTwoLineHeader(FixedWidthHeader):

    a white-space delimited format line, so splitting on white space is
    needed.
    """

class FixedWidthTwoLineDataSplitter(FixedWidthSplitter):

class FixedWidthTwoLineData(FixedWidthData):

class FixedWidthTwoLine(FixedWidth):
    """Fixed width table which has two header lines.

    The first header line defines the column names and the second implicitly
    defines the column positions.

    Examples::

      # Typical case with column extent defined by ---- under column names.

       col1    col2         <== header_start = 0
      -----  ------------   <== position_line = 1, position_char = "-"
        1     bee flies     <== data_start = 2
        2     fish swims

      # Pretty-printed table

      +------+------------+
      | Col1 |   Col2     |
      +------+------------+
      |  1.2 | "hello"    |
      |  2.4 | there world|
      +------+------------+

    See the :ref:`astropy:fixed_width_gallery` for specific usage examples.

    """

    _format_name = "fixed_width_two_line"
    _description = "Fixed width with second header line"
    data_class = FixedWidthTwoLineData
    header_class = FixedWidthTwoLineHeader

    def __init__(
        self,
        position_line=None,
        position_char="-",
        delimiter_pad=None,
        bookend=False,
        header_rows=None,
    ):
        if len(position_char) != 1:
            raise ValueError(
                f'Position_char="{position_char}" must be a single character'
            )
        super().__init__(
            delimiter_pad=delimiter_pad, bookend=bookend, header_rows=header_rows
        )
        if position_line is None:
            position_line = len(self.header.header_rows)

        Note that this will *not* be called if the row already matches the header.

        Parameters
        ----------
        str_vals : list
            A list of value strings from the current row of the table.
        ncols : int

Thought: Checkpoint 1 — Identify the bug

- What I extracted from the report (entities / stack):
  - The stack trace shows Writer(...) invocation ended up calling RST.__init__ and Python raised TypeError: RST.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'header_rows'.
  - The failing call site was core._get_writer(...); the user was calling tbl.write(..., format='ascii.rst', header_rows=[...]) so the writer factory passed header_rows into the writer constructor.

- What I searched for and read to start:
  - Located RST class in astropy/io/ascii/rst.py (search for "class RST").
  - Opened the full file astropy/io/ascii/rst.py and astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py (FixedWidth parent and related classes) and astropy/io/ascii/basic.py to follow read/write behavior.

- Primary responsible class / method:
  - Root cause: RST.__init__ does not accept header_rows and therefore the writer factory calling Writer(**writer_kwargs) fails with TypeError.
  - The primary class responsible: astropy.io.ascii.rst.RST (its __init__ method).
  - Secondary problem: RST.write assumes a single header row when deciding which line to duplicate as the top/bottom RST border (it hard-codes lines[1]). That breaks semantics when header_rows has more than one element.

Concrete evidence from source:

- astropy/io/ascii/rst.py (entire file) — the RST class:

  - RST currently defines:
    - class attributes: _format_name, _description, data_class = SimpleRSTData, header_class = SimpleRSTHeader
    - __init__(self): calls super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False) — note: does not accept header_rows
    - write(self, lines): calls lines = super().write(lines) then lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]] and returns lines. This uses index 1 unconditionally.

- astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py — FixedWidth.__init__ signature:
  - FixedWidth.__init__(..., header_rows=None) accepts header_rows and sets header.header_rows and data.header_rows; if data.start_line is None it sets data.start_line = len(header_rows).
  - FixedWidthData.write uses header_rows and always appends the "position_line" (the line of repeated position_char) after the header rows (if header.position_line is not None).

Clear statement of bug:
- RST writer does not accept the header_rows keyword (TypeError). Also, even if we enabled header_rows, RST.write currently assumes there is only one header row (it uses lines[1] as the position/border line), so multi-line headers would produce incorrect borders. Thus a two-part fix is needed:
  1) Accept header_rows argument in RST.__init__ and forward it to FixedWidth.__init__.
  2) Make RST.write compute which line in the result is the "position" (border) line dynamically based on the actual header_rows, and ensure the data start line (for reading) is consistent with header_rows length.

Checkpoint 2 — Read the primary file and catalog everything

     - start_line = 1
     - splitter_class = DefaultSplitter
     - position_char = "="
   - Methods:
     - get_fixedwidth_params(self, line): calls super().get_fixedwidth_params(line), then sets ends[-1] = None to allow the rightmost column to be unbounded. (This is a small tweak to the fixed-width splitting logic.)

   - Notes: position_line = 0 and start_line = 1 encode the typical reStructuredText simple-table structure: top position/border at line 0, header names at line 1. position_char = "=" is the RST border character.

2) class SimpleRSTData(FixedWidthData)
   - Parent: FixedWidthData
   - Class-level attributes:
     - start_line = 3
     - end_line = -1

   - Methods: none defined in this file.

   - Notes: start_line = 3 encodes the convention that by default data starts at line index 3 (0: top border, 1: name, 2: border between header and data, 3: first data line) for the default single header row case.

3) class RST(FixedWidth)

     - write(self, lines): calls super().write(lines) and then wraps the result using lines[1] as the border line: lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]

  - SimpleRSTHeader.position_char = "="
  - SimpleRSTData.start_line = 3       (data start line for default single header row)
  - SimpleRSTData.end_line = -1

Checkpoint 3 — Map the architecture

3a) Inspect component classes in full (done above)
- Hardcoded values and what they represent:
  - position_line = 0: position line used to find column positions is at absolute line index 0 in the file (the top border row).
  - start_line = 1: header names begin at line index 1.
  - position_char = "=": the character used in the position line (border).
  - SimpleRSTData.start_line = 3: data is expected by default to start at line index 3 (top border (0), header name (1), middle border (2), so data at 3). This encodes the assumption that there is exactly one header row ("name") between the top and middle border lines.
  - end_line = -1: standard meaning of reading until end.

3b) Parent class (FixedWidth) — list ALL methods it defines and which RST overrides:
- FixedWidth (astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py) defines:
  - __init__(self, col_starts=None, col_ends=None, delimiter_pad=" ", bookend=True, header_rows=None)
    - It sets defaults for header_rows and configures header and data objects:
      - sets header.header_rows = header_rows
      - sets data.header_rows = header_rows
      - if data.start_line is None: data.start_line = len(header_rows)
      - sets splitter delimiters and bookend flags
  - No other methods are defined directly on FixedWidth (writing is handled by FixedWidthData.write and header write methods).
- RST overrides:
  - __init__ (currently with no args) — this is the one causing the TypeError because it does not accept header_rows.
  - write — RST overrides write to add the top and bottom border lines, but currently assumes single header row by using lines[1] unconditionally.

3c) Sibling classes (other classes inheriting FixedWidth) and how they handle header_rows:
- FixedWidthTwoLine (in fixedwidth.py) defines an __init__ signature that accepts header_rows and passes header_rows to FixedWidth.__init__:
  - def __init__(..., header_rows=None):
       super().__init__(delimiter_pad=..., bookend=..., header_rows=header_rows)
    - After the super call, it sets self.data.start_line based on position_line and header.header_rows; specifically: if position_line is None: position_line = len(self.header.header_rows); self.data.start_line = position_line + 1 — i.e., it adjusts data.start_line to be consistent with header_rows.
- FixedWidthNoHeader passes header_rows=[] to super() in its __init__.

This shows the correct pattern: subclasses accept header_rows (forward them to FixedWidth.__init__) and then compute/adjust self.header.position_line / self.data.start_line if needed.

3d) Call graph around RST — get_subgraph failed earlier, but we traced call sites from the stacktrace: writer factory calls Writer(**writer_kwargs) with header_rows included. Basic write flow:
- basic.Basic.write / BasicHeader/BasicData manage writing header and data lines.
- FixedWidthData.write forms hdrs_list by iterating header_rows and then appends a "position_line" after the header lines when header.position_line is not None.
- RST.write currently takes the position line (assumed at index 1) from this output and duplicates it at the top and bottom.

Checkpoint 4 — Propose a fix, then challenge it

Proposed fix (summary):
1) Modify astropy/io/ascii/rst.py RST.__init__ to accept the header_rows kwarg and forward it to FixedWidth.__init__. Example signature: def __init__(self, header_rows=None): super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=header_rows)
2) After calling super().__init__(...), compute and set the data.start_line appropriate for RST format with arbitrary header_rows length:
   - self.data.start_line = self.header.start_line + len(self.header.header_rows) + 1
   (This ensures data starts after the top border, the header rows, and the position/border line that follows header rows.)
3) Update RST.write to compute which line in the list returned from super().write is the position/border line (that should be duplicated as top and bottom). Do not use a hard-coded lines[1]. Instead, compute border_index = len(self.header.header_rows) (since FixedWidthData.write appends the position line after header rows), and use lines[border_index] (with safe fallback if lines shorter than expected).

Concrete code patch for astropy/io/ascii/rst.py (replace current __init__ and write):

Current code:
  def __init__(self):
      super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)

  def write(self, lines):
      lines = super().write(lines)
      lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]
      return lines

Proposed replacement:
  def __init__(self, header_rows=None):
      # Forward header_rows to FixedWidth so header/data objects are configured.
      super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=header_rows)
      # For reStructuredText the data start is after:
      #   - top position line (index 0),
      #   - header rows (header.start_line ..),
      #   - the position line that follows header rows.
      # Compute and set data.start_line so reading will match writing for any
      # header_rows length.
      self.data.start_line = self.header.start_line + len(self.header.header_rows) + 1

  def write(self, lines):
      lines = super().write(lines)
      # FixedWidthData.write generates header rows (len = len(header.header_rows))
      # and then appends a position line if header.position_line is not None.
      # That position line is at index len(header.header_rows) in the list of lines
      # returned by super().write. Use it as the border to duplicate at top & bottom.
      hdr_count = len(getattr(self.header, "header_rows", []))
      if self.header.position_line is not None:
          border_idx = hdr_count
      else:
          border_idx = 0
      # Safe fallback if output is shorter than expected
      if not lines:
          return lines
      if border_idx >= len(lines):
          # fallback to the last available position-like line (best-effort)
          border_idx = min(len(lines) - 1, border_idx)
      border_line = lines[border_idx]
      return [border_line] + lines + [border_line]

Now, challenge this fix through the required questions:

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- The current code assumes RST has a fixed single header row and that RST.__init__ need not accept header_rows; the fix removes that assumption and makes header rows count configurable and used consistently by write and by setting the correct data start index.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- SimpleRSTHeader.position_line = 0 and SimpleRSTHeader.start_line = 1 encode absolute positions for the typical RST pattern (top position/border at index 0, headers start at index 1). These remain correct for RST regardless of number of header rows (header rows start at 1).
- SimpleRSTData.start_line = 3 currently encodes the value for the default single header case (data starts at line 3: 0=top border, 1=name, 2=middle border, 3=data). If we allow header_rows != ["name"], this fixed start_line (3) is not generally correct. Therefore the hardcoded SimpleRSTData.start_line must be reconciled with dynamic header_rows handling.
  - My fix makes data.start_line dynamic in RST.__init__ (self.data.start_line = self.header.start_line + len(self.header.header_rows) + 1), so this hardcoded 3 will be overridden by the RST constructor and thus is no longer a problem at the RST-level.
- SimpleRSTHeader.position_char = "=" is fine and remains correct for RST.
- Any other numeric constants: the line indexing logic in FixedWidthData.write implicitly depends on header_rows length when it appends the position line after hdrs_list; RST.write assumed lines[1] (single header row), we replace that with dynamic computation.

Conclusion for 4b: the only hardcoded start_line that needed to be made effectively dynamic at the RST level was SimpleRSTData.start_line (was 3). The safe, minimal change is to leave SimpleRSTData.start_line as-is (so that the module-level default remains consistent) but override it at runtime in RST.__init__ so reading and writing will be correct when RST is used. Alternatively, one could change SimpleRSTData.start_line to None and rely on FixedWidth.__init__ to set data.start_line, but FixedWidth.__init__ sets it to len(header_rows), which is not the right formula for RST (it doesn't account for top border and the extra position line). So the runtime override is preferred.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?" (reading)
- We must ensure reading still works. The parent FixedWidth and FixedWidthHeader.get_cols use header.start_line and header.position_line to parse header names and column positions. Key points:
  - SimpleRSTHeader.position_line = 0, start_line = 1: reading will find the position line (top border) at index 0 and header names at index 1 + header_rows.index("name"), etc.
  - FixedWidthData.read uses self.data.start_line to slice data correctly. We changed self.data.start_line at construction to self.header.start_line + len(self.header.header_rows) + 1 which is the correct formula for RST with arbitrary header_rows:
    - Example: header_rows = ["name"] -> data.start_line = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 (original default)
    - header_rows = ["name","unit"] -> data.start_line = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 (correct: top border at 0, name at 1, unit at 2, position_line at 3, data at 4)
  - Therefore, reading code (FixedWidthHeader.get_cols and FixedWidthData.get_data_lines logic in base classes) will continue to work with the new dynamic data.start_line.

- Does the parent or other inherited methods need overriding because of this change?
  - FixedWidth.__init__ already accepts header_rows and sets header.header_rows and data.header_rows. We are now using that. We also set data.start_line explicitly in RST.__init__ (overriding the prior hard-coded SimpleRSTData.start_line for runtime use). No additional overrides of parent methods are required.
  - In particular, the parent's write/read methods operate on header.header_rows and data.start_line; we updated those, so correct behavior follows.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- SimpleRSTHeader.get_fixedwidth_params: unchanged and still fine.
- SimpleRSTData: no methods were changed. Its class attribute start_line remains 3 but will be overridden by RST.__init__ at runtime; reading/writing via RST will use the dynamically-set start_line.
- RST.__init__: changed to accept header_rows and to set data.start_line properly.
- RST.write: changed to use computed border index rather than hardcoded index 1; ensures correct top/mid/bottom border placement for arbitrary header_rows length.
- Other FixedWidth methods and Basic.* code: no changes required, but we checked that FixedWidthData.write produces hdrs_list followed by position line; RST.write then picks that position line index = len(header.header_rows). This is consistent with the FixedWidthData.write behavior.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods required. We only modify RST.__init__ and RST.write. We do not need to add read() or write_header() overrides because FixedWidthHeader.get_cols and FixedWidthData.write already handle header_rows semantics; we simply ensure RST uses the right lines for borders and sets a correct data start for reading.

Edge cases considered and handled by the proposed change:
- If lines returned by super().write are shorter than expected (unexpected formatting), the write implementation falls back to available lines rather than raising IndexError (safe fallback).
- If header.position_line is None (unlikely for RST but guarded for safety), the border index falls back to 0.
- For default behavior (no header_rows passed), behavior remains identical to prior behavior (header_rows defaults to ['name'] in FixedWidth.__init__, header.start_line=1 -> data.start_line will be 3, and RST.write picks border_idx = 1 as before).

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (complete analysis and exact changes needed)

Root cause:
- RST.__init__ did not accept and forward the header_rows keyword, so writer factory calling Writer(header_rows=...) raised TypeError. Additionally, RST.write assumed there was exactly one header row and used a hard-coded index lines[1] as the position/border line; that would be incorrect for multi-line headers even if RST.__init__ accepted header_rows.

All locations that need change:
1) astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
   - RST.__init__ — change signature to accept header_rows and forward it to FixedWidth.__init__; then compute and set self.data.start_line based on header.start_line and actual header_rows length.
   - RST.write — change to select the border line dynamically using header.header_rows length, with safe fallbacks.

No other files need changes for the fix itself, but to be thorough we list relevant code paths that we reviewed and validated:
- astropy/io/ascii/fixedwidth.py (inspected but not modified): FixedWidth.__init__ accepts header_rows and sets header.header_rows and data.header_rows; FixedWidthData.write appends the position line after header rows. These behaviors are relied upon.
- No changes required to SimpleRSTHeader or SimpleRSTData definitions; instead we override data.start_line at RST construction time so that read/write remain consistent for arbitrary header_rows.

Why the proposed changes are correct:
- They accept the header_rows parameter as other FixedWidth-derived writers do (matching sibling pattern in FixedWidthTwoLine).
- They make RST.write pick the correct border line that FixedWidthData.write produced (position line always comes right after header rows in the list returned by super().write).
- They set self.data.start_line to the correct index for reading to match writing for any number of header_rows:
    data.start_line = header.start_line + len(header.header_rows) + 1
  (start_line is the index where header.name begins; add number of header rows; add 1 for the position/border line that follows header rows).

Concrete patch (diff-like description)
- In astropy/io/ascii/rst.py:

Replace:

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)

    def write(self, lines):
        lines = super().write(lines)
        lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]
        return lines

With:

    def __init__(self, header_rows=None):
        # Forward header_rows so the header/data objects are configured
        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=header_rows)
        # For RST simple tables the data starts after top position line (index 0),
        # the header rows (starting at header.start_line) and the position
        # line that follows the header rows.
        # Compute that dynamically so reading matches writing for any header_rows.
        self.data.start_line = self.header.start_line + len(self.header.header_rows) + 1

    def write(self, lines):
        lines = super().write(lines)
        hdr_count = len(getattr(self.header, "header_rows", []))
        # The position/border line written by FixedWidthData.write appears after
        # the header rows so its index in `lines` is hdr_count.
        if self.header.position_line is not None:
            border_idx = hdr_count
        else:
            border_idx = 0
        if not lines:
            return lines
        if border_idx >= len(lines):
            border_idx = max(0, min(border_idx, len(lines) - 1))
        border_line = lines[border_idx]
        return [border_line] + lines + [border_line]

Tests to add (recommended):
- Write a small test that creates a table and calls tbl.write(sys.stdout, format='ascii.rst', header_rows=['name','unit']) and validates the produced lines match expected RST formatting with three border lines and header with two rows (top border; name; unit; middle border; data; bottom border).
- A read-write roundtrip test: write with header_rows=['name','unit'] and read back via RST reader to assert that data lines are parsed correctly (i.e., no mismatch in data.start_line).
- A regression test that ensures existing behavior for default header_rows (single name row) is unchanged.

Why I did not modify SimpleRSTData.start_line at the class level:
- Changing SimpleRSTData.start_line = None and relying upon FixedWidth.__init__ is not sufficient because FixedWidth.__init__ would set data.start_line = len(header_rows) which does not account for the presence of the top border and the position line that follows header rows in RST format. So better to compute the RST-specific data.start_line at RST instantiation time (we do that in RST.__init__). This is minimally invasive and keeps SimpleRSTData definition intact for other possible uses.

Wrap-up
- The minimal, complete fix is limited to astropy/io/ascii/rst.py:
  - accept header_rows in RST.__init__ and forward it to FixedWidth.__init__,
  - compute and set data.start_line dynamically to match header_rows length,
  - compute the border index in RST.write from header.header_rows length instead of hard-coded lines[1], with safe fallbacks.

This resolves:
- The TypeError when header_rows is passed to ascii.rst writer,
- Correct placement of top/middle/bottom borders when header_rows contains more than the default single row.

If you want, I can prepare an actual patch/PR (diff) ready to apply with the exact code changes (and tests) as described.
